statement: The Gallup Þjóðarpúls March 2026 survey found notable demographic patterns in EU referendum attitudes. Women were more supportive of continuing negotiations than men. Residents of the capital area (Höfuðborgarsvæðið) were more supportive than those in rural areas. University-educated respondents were more supportive than those with less formal education. By party affiliation, voters of Viðreisn and Samfylkingin were most supportive of resuming negotiations; Miðflokkurinn and Sjálfstæðisflokkurinn voters were most opposed. A notable finding was that nearly all respondents who oppose holding the referendum would vote to stop negotiations if one were held, but a significant minority who support holding the referendum would also vote against continuing negotiations — indicating that support for democratic process does not equate to support for the policy outcome.
caveats: Specific percentage breakdowns by demographic group are not included here as they were not available in the public summary. The demographic patterns (gender, education, urban/rural) are consistent with EU attitude surveys across Nordic countries and may reflect underlying structural factors rather than campaign effects. Party-affiliation breakdowns should be interpreted cautiously as some parties have small voter bases in the sample.

related_entries.statement: The Gallup Þjóðarpúls survey on the EU referendum was conducted online from 19 February to 4 March 2026 with a total sample size of 1,898 respondents randomly selected from Gallup's opinion panel. The response rate was 42.7%. The survey asked two main questions: whether respondents support holding a referendum on continuing EU accession negotiations, and how they would likely vote. Key headline findings: 57% support holding the referendum, approximately 30% oppose, and roughly 12% are neutral or undecided. On voting intention if a referendum were held: support for resuming negotiations leads, but when asked about actual EU membership (as opposed to negotiations), the split is closer to 42%–42% with 16% undecided. The gap between support for negotiations and support for membership is a significant methodological distinction in interpreting the results.

The Althingi (Icelandic parliament) has 63 seats. As of session 157, 57 seats are held by sitting MPs. The party breakdown is: Sjálfstæðisflokkur (Independence Party) 13, Samfylkingin (Social Democrats) 13, Viðreisn (Reform Party) 11, Miðflokkurinn (Centre Party) 8, Flokkur fólksins (People's Party) 8, Framsóknarflokkur (Progressive Party) 4. The remaining 6 seats are vacant or held by alternates (varamenn).

Miðflokkurinn (Centre Party), led by Sigmundur Davíð Gunnlaugsson, is aligned with the European Conservatives and Reformists (ECR) grouping and is strongly opposed to EU integration. In the context of the 2026 EU referendum campaign, Norwegian outlet Altinget.no reported that Miðflokkurinn representatives had met with US Republican Party contacts, and that a political advisor linked to Nigel Farage visited Iceland twice in early 2026. Heimildin also reported on these international connections. Sigmundur Davíð has cultivated ties with international conservative and Eurosceptic movements since founding the party in 2017 after splitting from the Progressive Party. The party holds 8 seats in Althingi as of session 157.

citations.report_title: Þjóðin á báðum áttum um viðræður: 52% gegn 48%

claims.claim_text: Karlar eru andvígari þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslunni en konur.
Íbúar höfuðborgarsvæðisins eru frekar hlynntir þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslu en íbúar landsbyggðarinnar.
Fólk með háskólamenntun er frekar hlynnt þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslu en fólk með minni menntun.
Kjósendur Viðreisnar og Samfýlkingarinnar eru helst hlynntir þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslu um framhald aðildarviðræðna.
Kjósendur Miðflokksins og Sjálfstæðisflokksins eru helst andvígir þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslu.
Konur myndu fremur kjósa með framhaldi aðildarviðræðna við ESB en karlar.
Íbúar höfuðborgarsvæðisins myndu frekar kjósa með framhaldi aðildarviðræðna en íbúar landsbyggðarinnar.
Fólk með háskólamenntun myndi frekar kjósa með framhaldi aðildarviðræðna en fólk með minni menntun.
Kjósendur Viðreisnar og Samfýlkingarinnar telja helst að þau myndu kjósa með framhaldi aðildarviðræðna fremur en kjósendur stjórnarandstöðuflokkanna.
Nánast allir sem eru andvígir þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslunni myndu kjósa með því að hætta aðildarviðræðum.
Þau sem eru hlynnt þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslunni telja frekar að þau myndu kjósa með framhaldi aðildarviðræðna.
Karlar eru fremur andvígir atkvæðagreiðslunni en konur.
Kjósendur Miðflokksins og Sjálfstæðisflokksins eru andvígastir þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslunni.
